question: What does ICMP stand for?
explanation: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) supports diagnostic tools like ping and tracert/traceroute.
aspect_key: ping; tracert; traceroute for reachability/path diagnostics
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 3/Week 3 Notes.md

question: Which delivery mode sends a frame to every device on the local network segment?
explanation: Broadcast delivers to all local hosts using destination MAC FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF.
aspect_key: one sender to all local hosts using destination mac ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 1 - Unicast

question: What does FCS stand for in an Ethernet frame?
explanation: FCS (Frame Check Sequence) is the Ethernet trailer field that carries the CRC for error detection.
aspect_key: frame-level corruption detection
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 2 - Ethernet Frame Structure/Frame Fields.md

question: In the TCP/IP model which layer maps to OSI Application Presentation and Session combined?
explanation: The TCP/IP Application layer collapses the three upper OSI layers into one.
aspect_key: tcp/ip application maps to osi application/presentation/session
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 3/Week 3 Notes.md

question: Which command-line tool tests basic network reachability using ICMP Echo Requests?
explanation: The ping command sends ICMP Echo Requests and reports whether a remote host responded.
aspect_key: ping; tracert; traceroute for reachability/path diagnostics
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 3/Week 3 Notes.md

question: Which delivery mode sends traffic from one sender to exactly one specific receiver?
explanation: Unicast is a one-to-one transmission — a single sender communicates with a single named receiver.
aspect_key: one sender to one receiver
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 1 - Unicast

question: Which media access method does Wi-Fi use to reduce collisions?
explanation: CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance) is used in wireless where collision detection is not feasible.
aspect_key: collision-avoidance access model used in wireless contexts
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 3 - CSMA-CD and CSMA-CA/CSMA-CA.md

question: What happens to MAC addresses when a packet crosses a router boundary?
choice_a: They are preserved end-to-end across the entire path
choice_b: They are replaced at each router hop (hop-local)
choice_c: They are permanently stripped and not used beyond the first router
choice_d: They are stored in the routing table for reuse
explanation: MAC addresses are hop-local — each router replaces source and destination MAC for the next link.
aspect_key: mac addresses are hop-local and do not traverse subnets end-to-end
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 3/Week 3 Notes.md

question: What is encapsulation in the context of networking?
choice_a: Compressing data to reduce transmission overhead
choice_b: Wrapping higher-layer data with lower-layer headers and trailers for transmission
choice_c: Encrypting packet payloads before they leave the host
choice_d: Splitting large frames into smaller segments
explanation: Encapsulation adds lower-layer headers and trailers around higher-layer data as it moves down the protocol stack.
aspect_key: higher-layer data wrapped by lower-layer headers/trailer for transmission
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 2 - Ethernet Frame Structure/Encapsulation.md

question: Which Ethernet frame fields appear before the data payload?
choice_c: Destination MAC; source MAC; and Type/Length
explanation: An Ethernet frame begins with destination MAC; source MAC; and Type/Length fields before the payload data.
aspect_key: destination mac; source mac; type/length; payload; fcs
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 2 - Ethernet Frame Structure/Frame Fields.md

question: What does CSMA/CD do after detecting a collision?
choice_a: Ignores the collision and retransmits immediately
choice_b: Waits a random backoff period before retransmitting
choice_d: Switches the affected port to full-duplex mode
explanation: CSMA/CD detects collisions then applies random backoff timing before retransmitting to reduce repeat collisions.
aspect_key: collision handling model for shared half-duplex ethernet media
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 3 - CSMA-CD and CSMA-CA/CSMA-CD.md

question: How does tracert identify each router hop along the network path?
choice_a: By performing a DNS lookup at each hop
choice_b: By sending packets with incrementing TTL values and collecting ICMP Time Exceeded replies
choice_c: By using ARP to discover each gateway
choice_d: By completing a TCP handshake with each router
explanation: tracert increments TTL by 1 per probe; each router that drops the packet sends an ICMP Time Exceeded revealing its address.
aspect_key: ping; tracert; traceroute for reachability/path diagnostics
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 3/Week 3 Notes.md

question: Why does Ethernet use CRC in the FCS trailer rather than a simple byte checksum?
choice_a: CRC is computed faster on modern silicon than a checksum
choice_b: CRC provides stronger mathematical error detection catching more burst error patterns than a simple sum
choice_c: CRC is required by the IPv6 specification for frame validation
choice_d: CRC also encrypts the frame payload for confidentiality
explanation: CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) uses polynomial division to detect a wider range of bit errors than a simple additive checksum.
aspect_key: frame-level corruption detection
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 2 - Ethernet Frame Structure/CRC Error Detection.md

question: What is the fundamental operational difference between CSMA/CD and CSMA/CA?
choice_a: CD is used on wireless; CA is used on wired Ethernet
choice_b: CD detects collisions after they occur and backs off; CA tries to avoid collisions before transmitting
choice_c: CD is used only on fiber links; CA applies to copper
choice_d: The two mechanisms are functionally identical in modern networks
explanation: CSMA/CD is reactive — it detects collisions after they happen. CSMA/CA is proactive — it listens and waits before sending.
aspect_key: collision handling model for shared half-duplex ethernet media
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 3 - CSMA-CD and CSMA-CA/CSMA-CD.md

question: Why is multicast more efficient than broadcast for video streaming to a subset of devices?
choice_a: Multicast sends fewer bytes per stream than unicast
choice_b: Multicast targets only subscribed receivers reducing unnecessary traffic on the network
choice_c: Multicast is limited to 10 Mbps so routers throttle it
choice_d: Multicast requires a dedicated VLAN which isolates it
explanation: Multicast delivers to a defined group — devices not subscribed never receive the frames; broadcast hits everyone.
aspect_key: one sender to selected group
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 1 - Unicast

question: In the TCP/IP model which layer encapsulates the OSI Data Link and Physical layers together?
explanation: TCP/IP Network Access (also called Link) layer covers the functions of both OSI Layer 1 and Layer 2.
aspect_key: tcp/ip network access maps to osi data link + physical
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 3/Week 3 Notes.md

question: Which part of an Ethernet frame does a NIC inspect first to decide whether to process or discard it?
explanation: The NIC reads the Destination MAC first — if it doesn't match the device's MAC or a broadcast it discards the frame.
aspect_key: destination mac; source mac; type/length; payload; fcs
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 2 - Ethernet Frame Structure/Frame Fields.md

question: What makes CSMA/CA's random backoff strategy effective at reducing repeated wireless collisions?
choice_a: It assigns fixed priority slots so no two devices transmit together
choice_b: Randomizing wait times makes it statistically unlikely that two devices retry at exactly the same moment
choice_c: It uses a shared token to grant transmission rights
choice_d: It disables transmission for all other devices until the collision is resolved
explanation: Random backoff distributes retry timing so colliding devices don't immediately retransmit simultaneously causing another collision.
aspect_key: collision-avoidance access model used in wireless contexts
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 3 - CSMA-CD and CSMA-CA/CSMA-CA.md

question: In the TCP/IP model which layer maps directly to the OSI Transport layer?
explanation: The TCP/IP Transport layer corresponds directly to OSI Layer 4 — both handle end-to-end delivery between hosts.
aspect_key: tcp/ip transport maps to osi transport
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 3/Week 3 Notes.md

question: In the TCP/IP model which layer corresponds to the OSI Network layer handling IP routing?
explanation: The TCP/IP Internet layer maps to OSI Layer 3 (Network) — both handle IP addressing and routing between networks.
aspect_key: tcp/ip internet maps to osi network
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 3/Week 3 Notes.md

question: Which delivery mode sends traffic from one sender to a selected group of subscribed receivers?
explanation: Multicast delivers from one source to a defined group of receivers — not to all (broadcast) and not to just one (unicast).
aspect_key: one sender to selected group
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 1 - Unicast

question: When a host needs to deliver a frame to another device on its local segment and knows only the destination IP what does it do first?
choice_a: Sends a DNS query to find the destination MAC address
choice_b: Sends an ARP broadcast to the segment asking which device owns that IP and awaits a MAC reply
choice_c: Opens a TCP connection and reads the source MAC from the handshake
choice_d: Looks up the destination MAC in the IP routing table
explanation: ARP broadcasts the target IP — the owning device replies with its MAC address enabling local frame delivery.
aspect_key: sends ip query and receives mac response on local segment
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 2 - Chapter 1 Protocols and Standards/TCP-IP Protocol Suite.md

question: What was RARP designed to solve in early networking?
choice_a: Resolving domain names before DNS was available
choice_b: Allowing a diskless device to broadcast its MAC address and receive an IP in return (legacy)
choice_c: Translating public IPs to private RFC1918 addresses
choice_d: Detecting and resolving IP address conflicts on a segment
explanation: RARP (Reverse ARP) let diskless hosts discover their IP by broadcasting their MAC — it was superseded by DHCP.
aspect_key: reverse request model from mac to ip (legacy context)
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 3/Week 3 Notes.md

question: In the TCP/IP model the Network Access layer combines which two OSI layers?
explanation: TCP/IP Network Access collapses OSI Layer 1 (Physical) and Layer 2 (Data Link) into a single layer.
aspect_key: tcp/ip network access maps to osi data link + physical
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 3/Week 3 Notes.md
